A narrative review approach is particularly appropriate for this research given the emergent nature of blockchain technology in supply chains and the need to understand various aspects of its implementation. Leveraging blockchain for enhanced transparency and traceability in sustainable supply chains
=============================================================================================

*   Review
*   [Open access](https://www.springernature.com/gp/open-research/about/the-fundamentals-of-open-access-and-open-research)
*    Published: 16 June 2025

*   Volume 3, article number 6, (2025) 
*   [Cite this article](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#citeas)

[Download PDF](https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7.pdf)

You have full access to this [open access](https://www.springernature.com/gp/open-research/about/the-fundamentals-of-open-access-and-open-research) article

[![Image 2](https://media.springernature.com/w72/springer-static/cover-hires/journal/44257?as=webp)Discover Analytics](https://link.springer.com/journal/44257)[Aims and scope](https://link.springer.com/journal/44257/aims-and-scope)[Submit manuscript](https://submission.nature.com/new-submission/44257/3)

 Leveraging blockchain for enhanced transparency and traceability in sustainable supply chains 

[Download PDF](https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7.pdf)

*   [Ben Chester Cheong](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#auth-Ben_Chester-Cheong-Aff1)[ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-1026-8292](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1026-8292)[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#Aff1)

Abstract
--------

The adoption of blockchain technology in supply chain management has gained significant attention due to its potential to enhance transparency, traceability, and accountability. However, successful blockchain implementation requires careful consideration of governance, regulatory, and ethical dimensions. Through a narrative literature review, this paper examines how blockchain can be effectively leveraged in sustainable supply chains, focussing on governance challenges and policy considerations. The review reveals significant gaps in current understanding of blockchain governance, including a lack of standardisation, limited integration between technical and institutional aspects, and insufficient attention to ethical implications. The analysis provides a comprehensive examination of the regulatory landscape, identifying critical areas requiring policy attention and suggesting pathways for implementation. The paper contributes to the literature by synthesising the current understanding of governance challenges and proposing policy directions for addressing these gaps. As blockchain technology continues to evolve, the paper emphasises the need for balanced governance approaches that promote innovation while ensuring responsible implementation across supply chain ecosystems.

[Use our pre-submission checklist](https://beta.springernature.com/pre-submission?journalId=44257)
Avoid common mistakes on your manuscript.

1 Introduction
--------------

Blockchain technology has emerged as a promising solution for enhancing the sustainability and resilience of supply chains, by enabling secure, transparent, and auditable record-keeping of transactions and information flows [[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR1 "Saberi S, Kouhizadeh M, Sarkis J, Shen L. Blockchain technology and its relationships to sustainable supply chain management. Int J Prod Res. 2019;57(7):2117–35.")]. The decentralised and immutable nature of blockchain can help to reduce the risks of fraud, corruption, and tampering, while also empowering supply chain actors to make more informed and ethical decisions [[2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR2 "Kouhizadeh M, Sarkis J. Blockchain practices, potentials, and perspectives in greening supply chains. Sustainability. 2018;10(10):3652.")]. However, the adoption of blockchain in supply chains is still in its early stages, and some significant challenges and barriers need to be addressed, such as the lack of standardisation, interoperability, and regulatory clarity [[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR3 "Wang Y, Han JH, Beynon-Davies P. Understanding blockchain technology for future supply chains: a systematic literature review and research agenda. Supply Chain Manag. 2019;24(1):62–84.")].

One of the critical factors for the successful implementation of blockchain in supply chains is the existence of an enabling institutional environment that promotes responsible and inclusive governance [[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR4 "Lim MK, Li Y, Wang C, Tseng ML. A literature review of blockchain technology applications in supply chains: a comprehensive analysis of themes, methodologies and industries. Comput Ind Eng. 2021;154: 107133.")]. Blockchain governance refers to the rules, processes, and mechanisms that guide the development, operation, and use of blockchain systems, as well as the roles and responsibilities of different stakeholders in the blockchain ecosystem [[5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR5 "Treiblmaier H. The impact of the blockchain on the supply chain: a theory-based research framework and a call for action. Supply Chain Manag. 2018;23(6):545–59.")]. Effective blockchain governance is essential for ensuring the integrity, security, and sustainability of blockchain-based solutions, as well as for promoting the fair and equitable distribution of benefits and risks among supply chain actors [[6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR6 "Venkatesh VG, Kang K, Wang B, Zhong RY, Zhang A. System architecture for blockchain based transparency of supply chain social sustainability. Robot Comput Integrated Manuf. 2020;63: 101896.")].

Despite the growing recognition of the importance of blockchain governance, there is still a lack of comprehensive understanding of how blockchain technology can be effectively governed in sustainable supply chains [[7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR7 "Paliwal V, Chandra S, Sharma S. Blockchain technology for sustainable supply chain management: a systematic literature review and a classification framework. Sustainability. 2020;12(18):7638.")]. Most of the existing research on blockchain in supply chains has focused on the technical and operational aspects of the technology, while the institutional and governance dimensions have been largely overlooked [[8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR8 "Köhler S, Pizzol M. Technology assessment of blockchain-based technologies in the food supply chain. J Clean Prod. 2020;269: 122193.")]. This paper aims to address this gap through a narrative literature review that examines how blockchain can be leveraged for enhanced transparency and traceability in sustainable supply chains.

A narrative review approach is particularly appropriate for this research given the emergent nature of blockchain technology in supply chains and the need to understand various aspects of its implementation. Unlike systematic reviews which are better suited for well-established fields with clearly defined parameters, narrative reviews allow for broader exploration of implementation challenges and governance implications. This approach enables the synthesis of current literature to identify key themes, challenges, and opportunities in blockchain adoption.

The paper makes several important contributions to the literature. First, it provides a review of blockchain applications in sustainable supply chains, identifying both opportunities and challenges. Second, it examines the governance considerations that need to be addressed for successful blockchain implementation. Third, it explores ethical implications that arise from blockchain deployment in supply chains. Through this analysis, the paper aims to provide insights for understanding how blockchain technology can be effectively leveraged while ensuring responsible and sustainable implementation.

2 Research objective, contributions and methodology
---------------------------------------------------

The main objective of this research is to examine how blockchain technology can be effectively leveraged for enhanced transparency and traceability in sustainable supply chains, with a particular focus on governance and ethical considerations. Through a narrative literature review, this paper aims to identify key challenges and opportunities in blockchain adoption, while exploring the governance implications and ethical considerations that arise from its implementation.

This research contributes to the growing body of literature on blockchain in sustainable supply chain management in several ways. First, it provides a narrative review of existing research on blockchain applications in supply chain management, identifying key themes, challenges, and opportunities. Second, it examines the governance considerations necessary for effective blockchain implementation, highlighting areas requiring particular attention. Third, it explores the ethical implications of blockchain deployment in supply chains, an aspect that has received limited attention in existing literature.

To achieve these research objectives, a narrative literature review methodology is adopted. This approach is particularly suitable given the emergent nature of blockchain technology in supply chains and the need to synthesise current understanding across multiple dimensions—technical, governance, and ethical. The narrative review allows for broader exploration and interpretation of the literature compared to systematic reviews, enabling the identification of patterns and insights that might not be apparent through more structured approaches.

The review encompasses academic publications from 2019 to 2024, with the inclusion of foundational papers from earlier periods where they establish key concepts. The analysis builds particularly on blockchain governance research by Saberi et al. [[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR1 "Saberi S, Kouhizadeh M, Sarkis J, Shen L. Blockchain technology and its relationships to sustainable supply chain management. Int J Prod Res. 2019;57(7):2117–35.")] and Chang et al. [[9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR9 "Chang Y, Iakovou E, Shi W. Blockchain in global supply chains and cross border trade: a critical synthesis of the state-of-the-art, challenges and opportunities. Int J Prod Res. 2020;58(7):2082–99.")], supply chain implementation studies by Kouhizadeh et al. [[10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR10 "Kouhizadeh M, Saberi S, Sarkis J. Blockchain technology and the sustainable supply chain: theoretically exploring adoption barriers. Int J Prod Econ. 2021;231: 107831.")] and Venkatesh et al. [[6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR6 "Venkatesh VG, Kang K, Wang B, Zhong RY, Zhang A. System architecture for blockchain based transparency of supply chain social sustainability. Robot Comput Integrated Manuf. 2020;63: 101896.")], and technological frameworks developed by Liu et al. [[11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR11 "Liu X, Barenji AV, Li Z, Montreuil B. Blockchain-based smart tracking and tracing platform for drug supply chain. Comput Ind Eng. 2021;161: 107669.")]. Through analysis of these works and others, the paper identifies key themes and gaps in current understanding while suggesting directions for future research and practice.

This methodological approach enables comprehensive coverage of blockchain’s potential in sustainable supply chains while acknowledging the limitations inherent in examining an evolving technology. The narrative review format allows for the exploration of nuanced relationships between technical capabilities, governance requirements, and ethical considerations that might be overlooked in more structured review approaches. The scope is limited to English-language academic publications analysing blockchain governance and supply chain applications. While this creates certain limitations in scope, it provides a focused analysis of current academic discourse on blockchain governance in sustainable supply chain management.

3 Literature review
-------------------

The growing body of literature on at the intersection of blockchain and sustainability highlights the potential of this technology to transform supply chain management practices and enable more sustainable and ethical operations.

### 3.1 Blockchain technology in sustainable supply chains: a foundational overview

Kouhizadeh et al. explore blockchain’s foundational role in sustainable supply chain management, emphasising its potential to enhance transparency, traceability, and accountability. They argue that blockchain’s decentralised, immutable, and transparent nature makes it well-suited for addressing modern supply network challenges characterised by complexity and opacity [[10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR10 "Kouhizadeh M, Saberi S, Sarkis J. Blockchain technology and the sustainable supply chain: theoretically exploring adoption barriers. Int J Prod Econ. 2021;231: 107831.")]. Supporting this view, Saberi et al. demonstrate how blockchain enables secure, decentralised record-keeping to verify sustainability claims and build stakeholder trust [[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR1 "Saberi S, Kouhizadeh M, Sarkis J, Shen L. Blockchain technology and its relationships to sustainable supply chain management. Int J Prod Res. 2019;57(7):2117–35.")]. Kshetri provides comprehensive evidence through real-world applications, such as IBM Food Trust’s end-to-end traceability and Everledger’s diamond provenance verification [[12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR12 "Kshetri N. Blockchain’s roles in meeting key supply chain management objectives. Int J Inf Manage. 2018;39:80–9.")]. Azzi et al. further emphasise blockchain’s capacity to create tamper-proof transaction ledgers while eliminating intermediaries, highlighting its crucial role in verifying sustainability claims and preventing greenwashing [[13](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR13 "Azzi R, Chamoun RK, Sokhn M. The power of a blockchain-based supply chain. Comput Ind Eng. 2019;135:582–92.")].

The literature presents diverse industry-specific implementations. Di Vaio and Varriale examine blockchain’s application in aviation supply chains, demonstrating improved efficiency and environmental performance through enhanced stakeholder coordination [[14](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR14 "Di Vaio A, Varriale L. Blockchain technology in supply chain management for sustainable performance: evidence from the airport industry. Int J Inf Manage. 2020;52: 102014.")]. In the food sector, Perboli et al. present case studies highlighting blockchain’s impact on traceability and food waste reduction [[15](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR15 "Perboli G, Musso S, Rosano M. Blockchain in logistics and supply chain: a lean approach for designing real-world use cases. IEEE Access. 2018;6:62018–28.")]. Liu et al. demonstrate blockchain’s application in pharmaceutical supply chains, addressing counterfeiting and regulatory compliance through smart tracking systems [[11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR11 "Liu X, Barenji AV, Li Z, Montreuil B. Blockchain-based smart tracking and tracing platform for drug supply chain. Comput Ind Eng. 2021;161: 107669.")]. Similarly, Natanelov et al. explore blockchain’s potential in supply chain finance within the beef industry, proposing frameworks for enhanced transparency and risk management [[16](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR16 "Natanelov V, Cao S, Foth M, Dulleck U. Blockchain smart contracts for supply chain finance: mapping the innovation potential in the Australian beef industry. J Ind Inf Integr. 2022;30: 100389.")].

### 3.2 Technical implementation and integration challenges

Chang et al. identify critical barriers to widespread adoption, particularly the lack of standardisation and interoperability between different blockchain platforms [[9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR9 "Chang Y, Iakovou E, Shi W. Blockchain in global supply chains and cross border trade: a critical synthesis of the state-of-the-art, challenges and opportunities. Int J Prod Res. 2020;58(7):2082–99.")]. Their analysis reveals how these technical limitations impede scalability and require significant coordination efforts among supply chain participants. Köhler and Pizzol demonstrate through case studies that blockchain functions not as a standalone solution but as part of an integrated technology system [[8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR8 "Köhler S, Pizzol M. Technology assessment of blockchain-based technologies in the food supply chain. J Clean Prod. 2020;269: 122193.")]. Their four-component framework analysing socio-technical aspects highlights the crucial interplay between technical capabilities and organisational factors. Kumar et al. analyse technical barriers in blockchain adoption, identifying scalability issues and standardisation challenges specifically in food supply chains [[17](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR17 "Kumar A, Liu R, Shan Z. Is blockchain a silver bullet for supply chain management? Technical challenges and research opportunities. Decis Sci. 2020;51(1):8–37.")]. Their examination of Hyperledger Fabric demonstrates how technical limitations intersect with organisational and regulatory requirements. Wong et al. complement this analysis through their study of SMEs, revealing how organisational readiness and external pressures influence successful blockchain integration [[18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR18 "Wong LW, Leong LY, Hoo JJ, Tan GWH, Ooi KB. Time to seize the digital evolution: adoption of blockchain in operations and supply chain management among Malaysian SMEs. Int J Inf Manage. 2020;52: 101997.")].

Recent research by Heidari et al. demonstrates how blockchain can be effectively integrated with federated learning to address data privacy challenges while enabling collaborative model development [[19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR19 "Heidari A, et al. A novel blockchain-based deepfake detection method using federated and deep learning models. Cogn Comput. 2024;16:1073–91.")]. Their findings highlight the importance of data normalisation techniques when implementing blockchain across diverse sources, suggesting similar considerations may be crucial for supply chain implementations. Particularly relevant is their approach to maintaining data privacy while enabling necessary information sharing among validated participants, a challenge that parallels supply chain transparency requirements.

### 3.3 Sustainability dimensions

Kouhizadeh et al. provide crucial insights into blockchain’s role in circular economy practices, proposing an integrated framework combining blockchain with IoT and big data analytics [[20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR20 "Kouhizadeh M, Zhu Q, Sarkis J. Blockchain and the circular economy: potential tensions and critical reflections from practice. Production Planning & Control. 2021;32(11–12):950–66.")]. However, they also raise important concerns about the energy consumption of certain blockchain consensus mechanisms, which could potentially undermine sustainability benefits [[10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR10 "Kouhizadeh M, Saberi S, Sarkis J. Blockchain technology and the sustainable supply chain: theoretically exploring adoption barriers. Int J Prod Econ. 2021;231: 107831.")]. Heidari et al. provide important insights on designing reliable data aggregation methods for industrial applications of blockchain [[21](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR21 "Heidari A, et al. A reliable method for data aggregation on the industrial internet of things using a hybrid optimization algorithm and density correlation degree. Clust Comput. 2024;27:7521–39.")]. Their hybrid optimisation approach demonstrates how blockchain systems can achieve both security and efficiency—a critical consideration for sustainable supply chain implementations. Their findings on density correlation and system reliability offer valuable lessons for supply chain applications, particularly regarding the balance between data accessibility and system security.

Helo and Shamsuzzoha demonstrate how blockchain integration with RFID and IoT technologies can enhance supply chain sustainability through improved tracking and resource optimisation [[22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR22 "Helo P, Shamsuzzoha A. Real-time supply chain—a blockchain architecture for project deliveries. Robot Comput Integrated Manuf. 2020;63: 101909.")]. Their work highlights the potential for technology convergence in achieving environmental objectives. Lim et al. present a comprehensive analysis of blockchain’s social sustainability implications, examining its potential to promote fair labour practices and consumer empowerment [[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR4 "Lim MK, Li Y, Wang C, Tseng ML. A literature review of blockchain technology applications in supply chains: a comprehensive analysis of themes, methodologies and industries. Comput Ind Eng. 2021;154: 107133.")]. Their review of literature from 2017 to 2020 reveals significant gaps in empirical evidence regarding blockchain’s social impact. Venkatesh et al. further explore social sustainability through their system architecture analysis, demonstrating how blockchain addresses fair pricing and labour exploitation issues [[6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR6 "Venkatesh VG, Kang K, Wang B, Zhong RY, Zhang A. System architecture for blockchain based transparency of supply chain social sustainability. Robot Comput Integrated Manuf. 2020;63: 101896.")]. However, they identify significant barriers including digital infrastructure limitations and resistance to change among supply chain actors.

### 3.4 Governance and stakeholder considerations

Tönnissen and Teuteberg analyse blockchain’s impact on supply chain actors through multiple case studies, revealing how the technology affects transparency and trust relationships [[23](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR23 "Tönnissen S, Teuteberg F. Analysing the impact of blockchain-technology for operations and supply chain management: an explanatory model drawn from multiple case studies. Int J Inf Manage. 2020;52: 101953.")]. Their work emphasises the importance of context-specific implementation approaches and stakeholder considerations. Zhou et al. investigate how blockchain-enabled traceability influences supply chain quality management, demonstrating improved operational and financial performance through enhanced stakeholder collaboration [[24](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR24 "Zhou X, Zhu Q, Xu Z. The mediating role of supply chain quality management for traceability and performance improvement: evidence among Chinese food firms. Int J Prod Econ. 2022;122(4):847–67.")]. Van Hoek draws parallels between blockchain and RFID adoption, cautioning against technology hype while identifying critical success factors including stakeholder collaboration and regulatory support [[25](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR25 "van Hoek R. Exploring blockchain implementation in the supply chain: learning from pioneers and RFID research. Int J Oper Product Manag. 2019;39(6–8):829–59.")]. This analysis is complemented by Verhoeven et al., who examine industry-specific implementation requirements and business model impacts [[26](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR26 "Verhoeven P, Sinn F, Herden TT. Examples from blockchain implementations in logistics and supply chain management: exploring the mindful use of a new technology. Logistics. 2018;2(3):20.")].

### 3.5 Unresolved questions and future research directions

The literature reveals significant gaps in the empirical validation of blockchain benefits. While conceptual frameworks abound, there is limited quantitative evidence demonstrating blockchain’s actual impact on supply chain sustainability. Future research needs to focus on developing concrete performance metrics and conducting large-scale empirical studies. The field remains fragmented, with limited cross-disciplinary collaboration. Future research should bridge gaps between computer science, operations management, and sustainability science to develop more holistic solutions for complex supply chain challenges.

The literature identifies significant opportunities in integrating blockchain with emerging technologies. While some studies explore potential synergies [[2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR2 "Kouhizadeh M, Sarkis J. Blockchain practices, potentials, and perspectives in greening supply chains. Sustainability. 2018;10(10):3652.")], there remains a notable gap in developing concrete frameworks for real-world implementation. Particularly, the integration of blockchain with artificial intelligence and IoT requires further investigation to create more intelligent and automated supply chain solutions. Paul et al. demonstrate this need through their analysis of the tea supply chain, highlighting how technological integration challenges particularly affect smallholder farmers and smaller enterprises [[27](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR27 "Paul T, Mondal S, Islam N, Rakshit S. The impact of blockchain technology on the tea supply chain and its sustainable performance. Technol Forecast Soc Chang. 2021;173: 121163.")]. Their findings emphasise the importance of addressing the digital divide while implementing integrated technological solutions.

A significant gap exists in measuring blockchain’s environmental and social impact [[7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR7 "Paliwal V, Chandra S, Sharma S. Blockchain technology for sustainable supply chain management: a systematic literature review and a classification framework. Sustainability. 2020;12(18):7638.")]. Most existing studies prioritise economic benefits such as cost reduction and efficiency gains, with limited attention to environmental and social performance metrics. This gap is particularly notable in developing standardised approaches to measure and verify sustainability claims through blockchain systems. While technical solutions for improving blockchain performance exist [[28](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44257-025-00032-7#ref-CR28 "Zheng Z, Xie S, Dai HN, Chen X, Wang H. Blockchain challenges and opportunities: a survey. Int J Web Grid Serv. 2018;14(4):352–75.")], the literature reve

--- SOURCE 2: Traceability and Blockchain: Transparency for Any Business ---
URL: https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/traceability-and-blockchain-transparency-for-any-business

SUMMARY:
# Traceability and Blockchain: Transparency for Any Business The immutability and transparency of blockchain make it an ideal technology for tracking and verifying the origins and movement of goods or data through supply chains. Permissionless blockchains, however, take traceability and transparency one step further and make all transactions public. The Cardano Foundation pursues traceability solutions that promote transparency, accountability, and trust across diverse use cases. The Cardano Explorer also enhances accessibility and traceability, allowing anyone to view the history of on-chain transactions. Such initiatives demonstrate blockchain technology's immense potential for enhancing traceability and transparency across diverse businesses. Using Cardano infrastructure, businesses can easily navigate the complexities of blockchain integration, unlocking the benefits of traceability solutions for a brighter future.

FULL CONTENT:

![cardano foundation logo](/_next/image?url=%2Fcardano-logo.svg&w=384&q=75)

# Traceability and Blockchain: Transparency for Any Business

![A professional headshot of Inês Botelho.](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fucarecdn.com%2Fa3e8c9a6-914e-49bd-9269-a7cd258592e5%2F&w=128&q=75)
![Cardano Foundation logo on top of an orange gradient, above a globe.](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fucarecdn.com%2F1fba840d-68ea-46de-b5d4-8c0dcc2c7e4a%2F&w=3840&q=100)
![Cardano Foundation logo on top of an orange gradient, above a globe.](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fucarecdn.com%2F1fba840d-68ea-46de-b5d4-8c0dcc2c7e4a%2F&w=3840&q=100)

An in depth look at the benefits of using blockchain to bolster traceability initiatives

As blockchain technology gains broader acceptance, many seek to understand its advantages as infrastructure. Similar to traditional ledgers, blockchains log transactions or data entries. Yet, they diverge from legacy databases by operating as decentralized ledgers, distributing transaction records across a network of computers—also frequently referred to as nodes—for tamper-proof, immutable storage.

Specifically, blockchain networks function as digital databases where transactions or data entries reside within blocks connected in a chain. Each block holds a unique cryptographic hash referencing earlier blocks, preventing alterations to earlier transactions without disrupting the entire chain. This crucial characteristic safeguards data integrity and security, making the infrastructure ideal for use cases requiring robust traceability.

## What is traceability?

Before delving into blockchain's role in strengthening traceability, let's define the concept. Traceability functions like a product's life story, enabling stakeholders to track its complete journey from creation to end consumer. It involves carefully recording every step the product takes—its origin, the ingredients or materials used, how it was handled and transported, and any changes along the way. These details connect to the product using unique identifiers, akin to how names tie a person to their individual history.

## Why is traceability important?

Traceability provides visibility and accountability throughout the product journey, validating safety, quality, and authenticity. In many ways, it serves as a safety net—if a product proves defective or contaminated, traceability allows for swift identification of the problem's source, minimizing damage. This safeguards consumers and helps companies avoid expensive recalls. It also helps to ensure that products adhere to quality standards and comply with regulations, an essential requirement in industries like food and pharmaceuticals.

Furthermore, traceability promotes trust among businesses within a supply chain, helping to streamline processes and eliminate inefficiencies by ensuring everyone has access to the same information. Businesses can also boost customer loyalty and product interest through increased transparency and brand relatability powered by traceability solutions. Notably, a growing number of consumers demand transparency as it empowers them to understand a product's origins, production methods, and alignment with environmental, humanitarian, and personal values. These insights foster informed decision-making and strengthen brand loyalty.

## How does blockchain traceability work?

The [immutability and transparency of blockchain](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/blockchain-basics-simple-guide-beginners) make it an ideal technology for tracking and verifying the origins and movement of goods or data through supply chains. On a more granular level, blockchain traceability works like a highly secure digital passport or stock keeping unit (SKU) for an item moving through a supply chain. Each step of the item's journey gets recorded in a unique information or data unit. The data units are then grouped together in blocks, which appear linked chronologically on a shared digital ledger—the blockchain. This ledger becomes practically unchangeable, and only those with authorized access can see it. Permissionless blockchains, however, take traceability and transparency one step further and make all transactions public. In permissioned blockchains, only a select group of participants can access the network, maintaining a degree of privacy. Conversely, anyone can view the transactions on permissionless blockchains, ensuring full transparency and promoting trustlessness.

Every change, like a new location, temperature reading, or transfer of ownership, is added to a block of transactions. This data generates a complete, immutable history. Blockchain also complements emerging technologies like AI and synthetic biology, both novel tools that benefit from verifiable infrastructure and credentials. As global leaders aim to maintain the integrity of global systems amidst such technological advances, transparency is more important than ever—especially in commerce, international trade, and governance. Such an undertaking needs openness, accountability, and flexible yet robust infrastructure. Cardano's potential becomes apparent considering these requirements.

## Leveraging traceability on Cardano

Cardano already supports decentralized applications (DApps) across diverse use cases, with a resilient infrastructure designed for transparency. Businesses can specifically leverage [Cardano's advanced features](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/introduction-cardano-blockchain) to bolster traceability initiatives, unlocking benefits such as:

## The Cardano Foundation’s traceability initiatives

The Cardano Foundation pursues traceability solutions that promote transparency, accountability, and trust across diverse use cases. With its secure and immutable ledger, the Cardano blockchain provides a suitable framework for recording and verifying the movement of goods and assets throughout their lifecycle. This enables enhanced supply chain management, product authentication, and counterfeit prevention, reinforcing customer engagement and brand reputation.

## Traceability from vine to glass

The Foundation collaborated with the wine industry in the [Republic of Georgia](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/cardano-foundation-partners-with-georgian-national-wine-agency), home of the world's oldest winemaking heritage. The [Baia's Wine and Scantrust](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/cardano-reveals-its-first-supply-chain-solution-in-association-with-scantrust) project initially tackled the challenges of counterfeiting and heavily paper-based export processes by digitizing procedures and linking them with digital signatures shared on the blockchain.

This decentralized approach means no single authority controls records, making the system resistant to censorship, tampering, and single points of failure. Furthermore, entries can be validated even if participants in the international supply chain access the information via different applications. Notably, the initiative to safeguard the authenticity of Baia's Wine through digital signatures resulted in an increase in sales across different international markets. Expanding the initiative with the National Wine Agency of Georgia, the Bolnisi Project today encompasses over 30 wineries in the Bolnisi region, preserving Georgia's rich winemaking legacy.

## Advantages of tokenizing physical goods

Similarly, the limited edition [proof-of-concept (POC) Hoodie](https://store.cardano.org/products/hoodie) from the [Cardano Store](https://store.cardano.org/) reinforces [traceability principles](https://store.cardano.org/pages/authenticated-products) by tokenizing each sweater using an NFC chip connected to a verification link. When scanned, the NFC retrieves the immutable blockchain record, authenticating the hoodie. Further highlighting traceability efforts, the Foundation had already [partnered with veritree](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/cardano-unveils-multiple-strategic-partnerships-at-the-cardano-summit-2021) during the Cardano Summit 2021 to establish the Cardano Forest. This collaboration involved the community sponsoring the planting of one million trees, connected to token records on Cardano's blockchain for transparent ownership verification and environmental impact tracking.

## Track and Trace with Cardano products

The Cardano Explorer also enhances accessibility and traceability, allowing anyone to view the history of on-chain transactions. The Explorer offers enhanced visualization of multiple components, promoting a better understanding of Cardano’s design decisions and on-chain transactions. Similarly, the Foundation’s Engineering Team has started to develop a Java-based data provisioning tool called [Ledger Sync](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/accessing-cardano-blockchain-data-with-ledger-sync), which simplifies access to Cardano blockchain data via a widely used programming language. With this easier access to data, users can trace network activity with more clarity and precision.

Such initiatives demonstrate blockchain technology's immense potential for enhancing traceability and transparency across diverse businesses. Companies can embrace blockchain solutions to improve operational efficiency, build stakeholder trust, and foster greater customer engagement and satisfaction. Using Cardano infrastructure, businesses can easily navigate the complexities of blockchain integration, unlocking the benefits of traceability solutions for a brighter future.

# How Blockchain Can Enhance Transparency, Traceability and Trust in Procurement Processes

![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAP///wAAACH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAICRAEAOw==)
![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAP///wAAACH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAICRAEAOw==)

**Editor’s note:** *This is the first in a series of 10 articles about advanced technologies and what they mean to procurement. Articles will run on the second and fourth Tuesdays of the month in* Inside Supply Management® Weekly*, Institute for Supply Management®’s (ISM®) e-newsletter. For more, see de Brabandt’s Technology column, “The Coming Consequential Differentiator That Is AI,” in the September/October issue of* Inside Supply Management®.

### \* \* \*

Supply management is a complex and interconnected process involving multiple stakeholders, transactions and data exchanges. Ensuring transparency, traceability and trust in procurement processes is vital for organizations to optimize efficiency, mitigate risks and build strong relationships with suppliers.

In recent years, blockchain technology has emerged as a game-changer, offering unprecedented capabilities to revolutionize supply management. This article, the first in a series of 10, explores how blockchain’s potential can benefit organizations.

### Enhanced Transparency

A significant advantage of blockchain is its ability to provide transparency in supply chain operations. Traditional systems often suffer from information asymmetry and a lack of visibility into the movement of goods, leading to inefficiencies and increased risks.

With blockchain, every transaction and interaction can be recorded on a distributed ledger that is accessible to all authorized participants in the network. This shared view of the supply chain ensures transparency by eliminating information silos and providing real-time visibility into processes, including procurement, manufacturing, transportation and delivery.

Blockchain also enables end-to-end traceability and provenance of products throughout the supply chain. By recording each transaction of the blockchain — including details such as origin, production, quality checks and ownership transfers — organizations can establish an immutable audit trail.

This traceability feature is particularly beneficial in industries where product authenticity and compliance with regulations are crucial, such as food, pharmaceuticals and luxury goods. Blockchain’s tamper-proof nature ensures that the information cannot be altered retroactively, ensuring the integrity and accuracy of product history.

Among blockchain’s other advantages:

**Streamlined procurement processes**, by eliminating intermediaries, reducing paperwork and automating transactions. Smart contracts — self-executing contracts with predefined rules encoded on the blockchain — can facilitate secure and efficient procurement transactions. These contracts can automate key procurement activities, including POs, delivery verification, invoicing and payment settlements. Smart contracts help streamline the procure-to-pay process, reduce manual errors, accelerate transaction speed and enhance efficiency for both buyers and suppliers.

**Improved trust and collaboration.** Blockchain’s decentralized nature, combined with its transparency and immutability, can foster trust and collaboration among supply chain participants. In traditional supply chains, trust is often built on relationships and intermediaries. However, blockchain eliminates the need for intermediaries by providing a secure and transparent platform for peer-to-peer transactions.

Smart contracts on the blockchain enable automated verification and enforcement of contractual terms, reducing the need for trust-based relationships. By leveraging blockchain, organizations can establish trust, enhance supplier relationships, and foster collaboration through shared data and processes.

**Enhanced supply chain security.** Supply chain security is a critical concern for organizations, particularly in mitigating risks associated with counterfeit products, fraud and unauthorized modifications. Blockchain provides an indisputable and auditable record of each transaction, making it extremely challenging for malicious actors to tamper with the data.

By storing product information, certifications and ownership records on the blockchain, organizations can ensure its integrity and reduce the risk of counterfeit goods entering the market. This enhanced security not only protects brand reputation but also safeguards consumer safety.

### Challenges and Considerations

While blockchain technology holds immense promise, several challenges need to be addressed before there is widespread adoption by supply management organizations. These include scalability, interoperability, data privacy, standardization and regulatory considerations. Organizations must also carefully evaluate the cost, technical complexity and readiness of their supply chain ecosystem before implementing blockchain solutions.

Blockchain technology has the potential to revolutionize supply management by enhancing transparency, traceability and trust in procurement processes. Through its transparent and immutable ledger, blockchain provides real-time visibility into supply chain operations, improves traceability, streamlines procurement processes, fosters trust and collaboration, and enhances supply chain security.

As organizations recognize blockchain’s transformative power, its adoption is poised to reshape the future of supply management, driving efficiency, sustainability and competitive advantage in procurement processes.

# Coinmetro

# The Role of Blockchain in Supply Chain Traceability

May 27, 2025

![How Blockchain Is Transforming Global Supply Chain Traceability](https://images.prismic.io/coinmetro-website/ZoHGvB5LeNNTwqNf_29JUN2024_1.png?auto=format%2Ccompress&fit=max&w=3840)

by Coinmetro Editorial Team

May 27, 2025

In 2021, a scandal rocked the food sector. Several suppliers had mislabeled expired meat and sold it as fresh. The result was public outrage and major financial loss for companies involved.

A report by McKinsey & Company found that fraud and inefficiencies in global supply chains cost over $2 trillion per year. This reflects the limits of legacy systems built on siloed data, manual entries, and poor oversight.

Supply chain traceability means tracking products, components, and materials from origin to final sale. It supports safety, authenticity, and regulatory compliance. Blockchain strengthens traceability by logging events on a shared, tamper-proof ledger. Each step—production, storage, and transit—is recorded with a timestamp and is visible to all approved parties. Smart contracts add automation. They enforce rules, halt risky shipments, and reduce manual errors. This blog will explore how blockchain improves traceability, builds trust, limits fraud, and drives smarter, faster supply chain decisions.

**This blog will outline:**

[**Discover The Future of Programmable Money: Smart Contracts in Finance**](https://coinmetro.com/learning-lab/the-future-of-programmable-money?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Supply chain traceability is vital for quality, compliance, and risk control. Traditional systems struggle to provide the speed, clarity, and accuracy that modern logistics require. Blockchain offers real-time tracking, data integrity, and shared visibility across all stakeholders.

By adopting blockchain, businesses enhance transparency, meet compliance demands, and protect supply chain integrity. Consumers also gain confidence, knowing the products they buy are safe, authentic, and properly handled.

**Lack of transparency:** When goods move through disconnected systems, errors and fraud become more likely. Without real-time tracking, inventory levels can be wrong. Shipments get lost, delayed, or misrouted, leading to higher costs and lower customer trust.

**Complexity of supply networks:** Modern supply chains involve many parties. Suppliers, manufacturers, shippers, and retailers often operate in silos. This lack of coordination slows down communication, increases mistakes, and makes it harder to resolve problems quickly.

**Regulatory compliance:** Industries like food, pharmaceuticals, and electronics face strict rules. These rules require accurate tracking across every step. Without a reliable system, meeting legal requirements is hard. Failure to comply risks fines, penalties, and brand damage.

Blockchain improves traceability by logging events on a tamper-proof, shared ledger. It verifies every movement and makes that data accessible. This reduces fraud, simplifies audits, and supports faster, more accurate decisions.

**Consumer trust:** Traceability builds trust by verifying a product’s source, quality, and authenticity. Today’s consumers expect proof that goods are safe, ethical, and accurately labeled. By tracing every step from origin to shelf, brands can confirm claims around sustainability and responsible sourcing. This visibility increases buyer confidence and long-term loyalty.

**Quality control:** Tracking products throughout the supply chain supports better quality control. With a full record of each step, companies can quickly detect issues and fix them. This level of oversight helps maintain consistency, reduce defects, and limit the risk of recalls or safety failures.

**Risk management:** Traceability improves recall readiness and protects against counterfeits. If a defect arises, firms can quickly locate and remove affected goods, limiting harm and legal exposure. Authentication at each stage also helps block fake or diverted products from reaching stores. That added control protects consumers and preserves brand integrity.

[**Learn About Companies & Banks Investing In Crypto & Blockchain**](https://coinmetro.com/blog/companies-banks-investing-in-crypto-blockchain?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

**Real-time tracking:** Blockchain allows real-time tracking of goods from origin to end consumer. Each transaction or movement of goods is recorded on the blockchain, providing a transparent and immutable ledger that all stakeholders can access. This real-time visibility ensures that everyone in the supply chain knows the product's status at any given moment, reducing the risk of errors and fraud.

**Shared ledger:** A shared ledger ensures all participants access the same information, reducing discrepancies. By using a decentralized database, blockchain eliminates the need for intermediaries to verify transactions, thus minimizing the potential for human error and conflicting records. This shared ledger approach enhances trust among stakeholders as they can independently verify the data's accuracy.

**Automated processes:** Smart contracts can automate and streamline processes, reducing manual intervention. These self-executing contracts are programmed to execute specific actions when certain conditions are met, such as releasing payment when goods are delivered. This automation reduces the need for manual checks and approvals, speeding up transactions and lowering administrative costs.

**Reduced delays:** Blockchain minimizes delays by providing a single source of truth for all participants. With all data stored on a decentralized ledger, multiple parties do not need to maintain and reconcile their records. This unified approach reduces the time spent verifying and validating information, ensuring that goods move through the supply chain more quickly and efficiently.

**Data integrity:** Blockchain's immutability ensures that recorded data cannot be tampered with. Once a transaction is recorded on the blockchain, it is nearly impossible to alter or delete, providing a secure and trustworthy record of all supply chain activities. This immutability helps maintain data integrity and prevent fraudulent activities.

**Fraud prevention:** Blockchain helps prevent fraud by providing a transparent and unalterable record of transactions. Every participant in the supply chain can view a product's entire history, from its origin to its current location. This transparency makes it difficult for bad actors to introduce counterfeit goods or manipulate records without detection.

[**Learn More About Using Blockchain to Drive Supply Chain Transparency**](https://www2.deloitte.com/us/en/pages/operations/articles/blockchain-supply-chain-innovation.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

![Infographic explaining blockchain applications in traceability, efficiency, and risk control](https://images.prismic.io/coinmetro-website/aDV0rCdWJ-7kSkMO_HowBlockchainEnhancesSupplyChain_additional.png?auto=format%2Ccompress&fit=max&w=3840)

**Overview:** Walmart uses IBM's Food Trust blockchain to trace food products from farm to store. This platform records every step of the food supply chain, from the farm where the food is grown through the processing and shipping stages to the store where it is sold.

**Impact**: The implementation of IBM's Food Trust has significantly improved transparency and reduced the time required to trace the origins of food products from days to mere seconds. This enhanced traceability helps Walmart ensure food safety, quickly identify and address issues, and build consumer trust by providing verifiable information about the products' origins.

**Overview**: Similar to Walmart, Carrefour, a multinational retail corporation, also uses IBM's Food Trust blockchain to track and verify the journey of its food products. This system enables Carrefour to trace a broad spectrum of agricultural products from the farm to the shelf.

**Impact**: Carrefour has increased transparency and consumer trust by utilizing blockchain technology. Customers can scan QR codes on products to access detailed information about the product’s journey, including origin, processing steps, and transportation details. This has enhanced food safety, ensured product authenticity, and improved customer experience.

**Overview**: Provenance is a platform that leverages blockchain technology to ensure the authenticity of products, particularly in the food and fashion industries. The platform tracks and verifies the journey of products from their origin to the consumer, providing a transparent view of the supply chain. Provenance works with companies like Unilever, Sainsbury's, and Patagonia to enhance transparency and traceability.

**Impact**: Using blockchain technology, Provenance significantly benefits companies and consumers. Companies gain improved transparency, which helps build consumer trust and enhance brand reputation. Consumers benefit from knowing the origins and authenticity of their products, ensuring ethical and sustainable practices are followed.

[**Opportunities and Challenges in Blockchain for Supply Chain Traceability**](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10182416?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

**Integration with Existing Systems:** Integrating blockchain into supply chain operations remains a major challenge. Most firms still run on legacy systems that were not designed for decentralized technology. These systems need substantial changes, including software upgrades, process redesign, and team retraining. Integration demands time, planning, and budget, and must avoid disrupting daily logistics.

**Scalability Issues:** Scalability limits blockchain’s use in high-volume supply chains. Many platforms cannot handle the number of transactions needed daily. Delays and congestion may arise when multiple parties submit data at once. To meet enterprise requirements, blockchain networks must evolve to process larger transaction loads quickly and reliably.

**Compliance with Regulations:** Unclear global regulations remain an obstacle to blockchain adoption in supply chains. Countries differ on rules for data storage, transmission, and blockchain use. Navigating these legal frameworks requires time, expertise, and strong compliance planning. Failing to meet legal obligations may lead to penalties and stalled deployment.

**Data Privacy Concerns:** Laws like the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) impact how blockchain can be used. Blockchains are immutable, which means once data is added, it cannot be altered or erased. This directly conflicts with GDPR’s right to erasure in some jurisdictions. Companies must design hybrid systems or off-chain solutions to comply with privacy laws while using blockchain’s benefits.

**Stakeholder Resistance:** Many stakeholders resist blockchain because they are used to legacy systems. Concerns about complexity, control, or job disruption often delay adoption. These doubts can stall deployment even when the benefits are clear. To succeed, companies must offer training and show measurable value to win stakeholder trust.

**Cost of Implementation:** Building blockchain infrastructure requires capital, time, and skilled labor. Custom development, integration, and security drive initial costs. Training staff and maintaining the system add further expense. To justify adoption, firms must weigh costs against blockchain’s long-term gains in speed, trust, and operational resilience.

The future of blockchain in supply chains looks strong. As technology evolves, we’ll see major advances in how companies track, verify, and optimize supply flows. Still, widespread global adoption will require solving key technical, operational, and regulatory hurdles.

Blockchain developers are improving scalability and speed. Solutions like sharding and layer-two protocols will allow networks to handle more transactions per second. This will make blockchain more usable for complex, high-volume supply chains.

More businesses recognize blockchain’s role in boosting supply chain efficiency and trust. Early adopters gain an edge through better visibility and fewer errors. As proven use cases grow, adoption will accelerate, driving even more innovation across industries.

Governments and regulators are beginning to offer clearer guidance for blockchain use. New rules will help companies stay compliant while using decentralized systems. Better clarity around data privacy, cross-border transfer, and storage will support broader implementation.

Today’s consumers want transparency in how products are sourced, made, and delivered. Blockchain meets this demand by offering a verifiable product history. Brands that deliver this visibility are more likely to earn loyalty and repeat business.

Upfront costs are high, but blockchain reduces waste, fraud, and paperwork. Over time, this leads to leaner operations, faster reconciliation, and lower administrative overhead. As ROI becomes clearer, blockchain will be seen as a strategic investment.

Blockchain solves critical challenges in global supply chains — enhancing trust, speed, and control. Businesses that invest early can build systems that are future-proof, efficient, and resilient. As leaders show what’s possible, the path to mainstream adoption becomes clearer. The companies moving now are shaping the future of supply chain transparency and accountability, offering consumers greater confidence in the authenticity, origin, and integrity of the products they choose.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Collaboration with policymakers ensures blockchain regulations are well-informed and aligned with industry needs, fostering innovation while maintaining compliance and ethical standards in supply chains.

Developing standardized protocols for blockchain implementation promotes interoperability and trust among stakeholders, enabling seamless integration across diverse supply chain networks and enhancing traceability.

A favorable regulatory landscape encourages blockchain adoption by reducing barriers to entry, ensuring data privacy, and facilitating collaboration among supply chain participants to enhance transparency.
